description:
You are acting as an observability engineer who has just finished tuning a set of
    JSON-formatted Grafana dashboards located in

        /home/user/projects/obs-dashboards/dashboards/

    containing two files:
        • cpu.json
        • memory.json

    Management wants you to put those dashboard definitions under a simple local
    backup policy and to leave an auditable trail of what you did.

    Your end-to-end goal consists of three clearly-defined steps:

    1.  Ensure there is a directory called
            /home/user/backups/
        (create it if it does not exist).

    2.  Create a gzip-compressed TAR archive of the COMPLETE
        dashboards directory (not just its files) and place the archive inside
        /home/user/backups/ with the **exact** name

            dashboards_backup.tar.gz

        • The archive must, when listed with
              tar -tzf /home/user/backups/dashboards_backup.tar.gz
          show the top-level folder "dashboards/" followed by the two JSON
          files, i.e. the listing MUST look **exactly** like:
              dashboards/
              dashboards/cpu.json
              dashboards/memory.json

    3.  Append one log entry to the text file
            /home/user/backup_logs/backup.log
        in the following STRICT format (single line, no extra spaces or line breaks):

            YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS - BACKUP_CREATED - /home/user/backups/dashboards_backup.tar.gz

        • Replace YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS with the current system date and time
          obtained from the command
              date '+%F %T'
        • If backup.log does not yet exist, your command sequence must
          create it automatically before writing the entry.

    SUCCESS CRITERIA (automated tests will check all of these):

        A. The file /home/user/backups/dashboards_backup.tar.gz exists.
        B. The archive’s internal structure exactly matches the tar listing shown above.
        C. The file /home/user/backup_logs/backup.log exists and its **last**
           (most recent) line follows the required timestamped format and path.

    You should accomplish everything with at most 3–4 standard Linux commands
    (mkdir, tar, echo, etc.). Use relative or absolute paths as you prefer, but
    the resulting files and archive names MUST be exactly as specified so the
    tests can find them.
truth:
Pre-existing items before the task starts:

        1. Directory  : /home/user/projects/obs-dashboards/dashboards/
        2. File       : /home/user/projects/obs-dashboards/dashboards/cpu.json
           Contents   : {"dashboard":"cpu","version":1}
        3. File       : /home/user/projects/obs-dashboards/dashboards/memory.json
           Contents   : {"dashboard":"memory","version":1}
        4. Directory  : /home/user/backup_logs/
           (backup.log does NOT exist yet.)
        5. Directory  : /home/user/backups/
           (may or may not exist; tests create it beforehand to avoid permission issues.)

    Expected final state after correct agent execution:

        1. Archive file
           /home/user/backups/dashboards_backup.tar.gz
           • tar -tzf must output EXACTLY:
                 dashboards/
                 dashboards/cpu.json
                 dashboards/memory.json

        2. Log file
           /home/user/backup_logs/backup.log
           • The LAST line must match regular expression
                 ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2} - BACKUP_CREATED - /home/user/backups/dashboards_backup.tar.gz$
           • Lines preceding it (if any) are ignored.
